1. Risk-Based Regulation

2. Ethical Standards and Certification

Adopt risk-based AI rules, light for research, strict for high-risk uses. Require model cards, provenance tracking, and conformity marks like “Canadian-Tested, Globally-Trusted.” Clear liability rules and in-house safety testing will make AI both responsible and competitive.

1. RISK-BASED FRAMEWORK (Like EU AI Act)
- Unacceptable: Social scoring, manipulation (banned)
- High-risk: Healthcare, hiring, finance, law enforcement (strict oversight)
- Limited: Chatbots, deepfakes (transparency required)
- Minimal: Games, spam filters (self-regulation)

2. MANDATORY REQUIREMENTS FOR HIGH-RISK AI
- Pre-deployment impact assessments
- Human oversight mechanisms
- Adversarial testing for bias/safety
- Audit trails (explainable decisions)
- Third-party certification before deployment

3. SECTORAL STANDARDS
- Healthcare: FDA-style approval for diagnostic AI
- Finance: Stress

ai-security-risks-and-mitigation.text: Canada faces AI-related security risks including autonomous weapons, cyberattacks, deepfake disinformation, and enhanced espionage. To mitigate these, it is pursuing national cybersecurity strategies, AI safety oversight, real-time cyber defense, international collaboration, and legislative measures to ensure accountability and resilience.

Emerging risks include sophisticated cyberattacks, deepfake-enabled influence campaigns, algorithmic cyberattacks, and adversarial model poisoning. Info-Tech recommends investing in AI incident regimes, continuous red-teaming, dual-use screening, and strategic foresight tools. Building resilience through layered, zero-trust architectures and international collaboration is necessary for mitigating security risks.

Emerging AI risks: adversarial attacks, data theft/poisoning, autonomous failures, deepfakes, dual-use misuse.

Mitigation: robust standards/testing, secure/federated data, red-teaming, regulation, and cybersecurity-focused AI talent.

Emerging AI security risks include deepfakes, data poisoning, cyberattacks powered by generative models, and misuse of AI in critical infrastructure or defence. Canada can mitigate threats through strong governance, national red-teaming networks, and secure data standards. Partnerships across government, industry, and academia are essential. For AI Future Leaders, embedding AI safety and ethics in K–12 and educator training prepares future talent to recognize and counter risks responsibly.

Gaps across the stack

Compute: scarce high-bandwidth GPU clusters; limited sovereign (Canada-resident) capacity with strong privacy/security postures; long lead times for power, cooling, and interconnect.

Data: fragmented custodian models (health, mobility, environment); uneven data quality/lineage; few production-grade shared corpora and evaluation sets; limited secure data enclaves for cross-org analysis.

	•	Compute
Scarcity of affordable GPU time for training and fine tuning. Queue times and price volatility. Limited access to validation and red-teaming environments.

Fragmented operational data in legacy ERPs and line-of-business apps. Few domain data trusts for health, construction, agriculture, marine, and energy. Weak data lineage, consent, and retention controls.
	•	Connectivity
Rural and industrial dead zones that block telemetry, edge inference, and remote supervision. Inconsistent last-mile reliability for clinics, plants, sites, and ports.

Regular security audits and certifications should be required to ensure compliance.Building Resilience into AI Systems:AI models must be designed

Canada should protect critical infrastructure, data, and AI models with simple, enforceable rules: encrypt by default, keep sensitive data in Canada, and require audits before deployment. We could build resilience into AI systems in the form of backup models, fail-safes, red-team testing, and rapid incident reporting) so that failures don’t take services down. And....we could collaborate with trusted allies to share threat, and new 'best practices'.

Treat AI like any other critical system - verify components, lock down identity and data paths, test models independently, practice failure. Diversify providers, keep keys and logs in Canada, give operators playbooks and kill-switches. Measure everything. That’s how Canada protects its infrastructure, data and models in the AI era.

We face an intractable problem that we probably cannot win [Canadian sovereign infrastructure competing against larger infrastructure that will out compete us, and/or is not sufficiently comparable]. We need to focus on niche market applications and bespoke solutions that are under the radar of the bigger players.

national-security-and-ai.text: Sovereign AI compute capacity will be essential for Canada's security and economic growth. To forecast the scale of infrastructure needed, both public and private sector demand must be considered. Governments will require significant compute power for applications in national security, healthcare, and climate research, while the private sector will demand resources to develop AI-driven solutions across industries. A mixed model of public and private collaboration, emphasized by investments in both high-performance computing and AI-specific infrastructure, will ensure Canada’s competitiveness.

Canada’s long-term security and economic growth depend on developing robust sovereign AI compute capacity that can sustain innovation across sectors while protecting national data assets. For healthcare innovators like LabOnlineBooking (LOB) which leverages predictive analytics, patient flow optimization, and data-driven scheduling reliable and sovereign compute infrastructure is essential to support real-time processing of sensitive health data under Canadian jurisdiction. Current reliance on foreign cloud providers poses strategic risks in data privacy, pricing control, and access during glo

We need enough capacity to train Canadian-scale foundational models and securely process sensitive national data (e.g., health, defense). This must include public-sector HPC clusters for classified work and commercially competitive, Canadian-owned cloud-based capacity for industry and government use.

sovereign-compute-capacity.text:
Canada's current AI compute capacity is approximately 41 petaFLOPS, representing about 1% of the United States 3,700 petaFLOPS. Given the rapid growth in AI compute demand, with projections indicating a doubling of AI supercomputer performance every nine months, Canada must significantly enhance its infrastructure to maintain competitiveness and ensure national security.

To achieve this, a phased approach is essential:
2030: Establish foundational infrastructure, targeting a capacity of 300 petaFLOPS, integrating advanced technologies and fostering innovation ecosystems.

2040: Reach 500 peta

Canada’s long-term prosperity and national security will depend on sufficient sovereign AI compute capacity—infrastructure that ensures data control, innovation independence, and equitable access across sectors. Demand for compute is projected to grow tenfold by 2030, driven by both public and private uses.

1. Economic and Security Imperatives.
AI compute is now as strategic as energy or telecommunications. To remain competitive, Canada should target 5–10% of global Tier-1 compute capacity per capita—enough to sustain domestic research, innovation, and critical systems resilience. This ensure

Canada needs enough sovereign compute to support research, innovation, and security while avoiding dependence on foreign infrastructure. Over the next 3 years, capacity should grow in three tiers:

1. Research and Innovation (Tier R).
Provide broad access for universities and SMEs—target 0.3–0.5 exaFLOP/s of mixed-precision compute by year 2, scaling to ~1 exaFLOP/s by year 3 (about 25,000–35,000 accelerators). Prioritize renewable energy, fair-share scheduling, and open access for public-interest projects.

2. Security and Critical Infrastructure (Tier S).
Maintain a smaller 0.1–0.2 exaFLOP/s
